intro: TypeScript is a strict superset of JavaScript, so most JavaScript code is valid TypeScript — but the value comes from adding type annotations. Converting JavaScript to TypeScript means introducing interface and type declarations, annotating function parameters and return types, replacing `any`-typed patterns with proper generics, and enabling strict null checks. The result catches entire classes of runtime bugs at compile time.

diffs.from: No types. Errors appear at runtime when values have unexpected shapes.
diffs.to: Static type checking catches shape mismatches at compile time.

diffs.from: IDE hints rely on JSDoc comments or type inference from assignment.
diffs.to: Rich autocomplete, refactoring, and error highlighting from the type graph.

diffs.from: Objects are plain. Duck typing is implicit and unchecked.
diffs.to: interface and type alias define explicit structural contracts.

diffs.from: Null/undefined errors are the most common runtime crash source.
diffs.to: strictNullChecks forces you to handle null before using a value.

diffs.from: Functions work on any type. No way to express parameterised behaviour.
diffs.to: Generics <T> let you write type-safe reusable utilities like Array<T>.

examples.title: Adding types to a function
examples.description: TypeScript parameter and return type annotations catch argument mismatches at build time.

examples.title: Extracting an interface from an API response
examples.description: Untyped fetch results gain compile-time shape guarantees through a response interface.

tips.title: Start with noImplicitAny
tips.body: Enable noImplicitAny in tsconfig first. This forces you to annotate any intentionally. From there, add strictNullChecks once nulls are handled.

tips.title: Replace any with unknown
tips.body: When you truly don't know a type, prefer unknown over any. unknown forces you to narrow the type before using it, preventing silent bugs.

tips.title: Extract interfaces from usage
tips.body: For every object shape passed to multiple functions, extract an interface. This is the highest-ROI refactor when migrating a JavaScript codebase.

tips.title: Use satisfies for literal objects
tips.body: The satisfies operator validates an object against a type without widening it. Ideal for config objects where you want both type safety and inferred literal types.
